rulings.Case Title: SECURITY NATIONAL VS STATE OF CALIFORNIA
rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Mtn To Tax Costs
rulings.Rulings: Matter on Calendar for Friday, August 9, 2013, Line 1, RESPONDENT CALIFORNIA COASTAL COMMISSION Ntc Of Mtn To Tax Costs Granted in part. $38,102.44 in costs are awarded to SNG, as set forth in exhibit 1 to the opposition papers. SNG is the prevailing party for purposes of the costs statutes. The disputed costs are not expressly prohibited from being recovered and thus are recoverable in the court's discretion provided that they are reasonable in amount and reasonably necessary to the conduct of the case. I exercise my discretion to award the disputed costs and I find those costs to be reasonably necessary to the conduct of the case because of the lengthy and complicated record, the large number of difficult and involved factual and legal issues, and the high degree of helpfulness of the parties' presentations, especially the graphics, in enabling me to gain a good grasp of the record and to understand the issues and the parties' positions on those issues. The reasonableness of the amount of the costs is a closer call. On first blush, the costs seem quite high. However, upon a full examination of the papers filed on this motion, I am persuaded that SNG has met its burden that the costs it incurred for the disputed items are market rate and thus reasonable in amount. The hearing will be at 9 am, not 9:30am. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/HK

rulings.Case Title: MARIANNE CUNNINGHAM et al VS. GOLDEN GATE HEALTH CENTER et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion Amd Motion For Terminating Sanctions, Or In The Alternative, To Compel Ptlf Deposition And For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, August 9, 2013, Line 1: Defendant KINDRED NURSING CENTER WEST LLC dba GOLDEN GATE HEALTHCARE CENTER'S Motion For Terminating Sanctions, Or In The Alternative, To Compel Plaintiff's Deposition And For Sanctions Pro Tem Judge Jeffrey Windsor, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. Defendant is to confirm whether Plaintiff appeared for the IME scheduled for 8/6/13. If Plaintiff failed to appear, Plaintiff is required to show cause why terminating sanctions or monetary sanctions should not be imposed. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: DOUGLAS RUSSELL VS. DPR CONSTRUCTION COMPANY, A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendants Martin Vegas And Dpr Construction Motion For Summary Judgment
rulings.Rulings: Matter on Calendar for Friday, August 9, 2013, Line 15, Defendants Martin Vegas And Dpr Construction Motion For Summary Judgment The motion for summary judgment is denied. The motion for summary adjudication is granted and denied in part. The Court denies the motion for summary adjudication as to the FEHA causes of action (causes of action 1-7 and 11-13) because there is a triable issue of material fact regarding whether a special employment relationship existed between plaintiff and defendant DPR. The evidence shows that: (1) Mr. Smith was plaintiff's supervisor on the site (Plaintiff's depo. 22, 38; Vegas depo. 27-28); (2) DPR needed to sign plaintiff's time cards for him to get paid (Plaintiff's depo. 96); (3) Mr. Vegas instructed Plaintiff on how to operate the elevator (Plaintiff's depo. 89-90; 153); (4) DPR provided locks for the elevator (Plaintiff's depo. 153); and (5) DPR set the work hours (Vegas depo. 136). "The common and prevailing principle espoused in all of the tests directs us to consider the 'totality of circumstances' that reflect upon the nature of the work relationship of the parties, with emphasis upon the extent to which the defendant controls the plaintiff's performance of employment duties." Vernon v. State of CA (2004) 116 Cal.App.4th 114, 124. The motion for summary adjudication is denied as to the claim for negligent retention as to defendant DPR. Plaintiff's deposition testimony at 93-96 and par. 4 of the Chisum declaration show that the abuse was pervasive and there is a triable issue of fact regarding whether defendant ought to have been aware of it. In addition, that evidence shows that plaintiff was instructed not to report incidents to his supervisor Mr. Smith. The motion for summary adjudication is granted as to defendant Vegas because he was not the employer and cannot retain himself. The motion for summary adjudication is denied as to the battery cause of action because there is a triable issue of material fact regarding whether ripping a back brace off of a disabled individual constitutes an offensive touching. (Plaintiff's depo. 92). Defendant fails to shift the burden on the intentional infliction of emotional distress cause of action. Plaintiff testified regarding severe emotional distress. (Plaintiff's depo. 165). The Court denies summary adjudication on the Unruh Act claim. If there is no employer-employee relationship between plaintiff and DPR, then the Unruh Act claim is an alternative remedy. See Payne v. Anaheim Memorial Medical Center 130 Cal.App.4th 729. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and in compliance with Code of Civil Procedure 437c(g) and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested.=302/PHA

rulings.Case Title: REAL ESTATE EBROKER, INC. VS. EDWARD LITKE et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on Calendar for Friday, August 9, 2013, Line 10, PLAINTIFF REAL ESTATE EBROKER, INC. MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Plaintiff's motion for summary judgment, or in the alternative, summary adjudication is denied as to the first and second causes of action in Plaintiff's Complaint. Defendant's request for judicial notice is granted. Plaintiff shifted the burden by producing affirmative evidence that it entered into an Exclusive Rental Listing Agreement ("Agreement") with Defendant Litke, and that Plaintiff was owed a commission fee if it procured a lessee for the property located at 838 Market Street. (See Declaration of Lanyadoo in Support of Plaintiff's Motion for Summary Judgment, or in the Alternative, Summary Adjudication ("Lanyadoo Declaration"), Exh. C.) Furthermore, Plaintiff has shown that it procured a lessee, and a lease was signed for the property at 838 Market Street on May 23, 2012. (See Lanyadoo Declaration, Exh. D.) As such, Plaintiff shifted the burden to Defendant to show that a triable issue of material fact exists. Defendant has produced sufficient evidence to show that a triable issue of fact exists regarding the schedule of payment in the Agreement. Based on the extrinsic evidence submitted by Defendant, the Court finds the term "negation" in the "Time of Payment" clause of the Agreement to be ambiguous as a matter of law. (See Declaration of Litke in Declaration in Opposition to Motion for Summary Judgment ("Litke Declaration"), 3-6.) However, where extrinsic or parol evidence is admitted that conflicts with the terms of the Agreement, resolving the conflict is a question of fact. (See Wolf v. Superior Court, 114 Cal. App. 4th 1343, 1351 (2004).) Here, the extrinsic evidence admitted in Litke's Declaration is parol evidence that conflicts with the terms of the "Time of Payment" clause. (See Litke Declaration 3-6.) Consequently, the "admission of the parol evidence creates a question of fact which precludes summary judgment." (Wolf, 114 Cal. App. 4th at 1351 (quoting Walter E. Heller Western, Inc. v Tecrim Corp. (1987 196 Cal. App. 3d 149, 158.) Plaintiff's motion for summary adjudication as to the second cause of action for common counts is denied as the same triable issue of fact remains regarding the schedule of payment of the commission fee. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and in compliance with Code of Civil Procedure 437c(g) and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/PHA

rulings.Case Title: WALTER YEE et al VS. THE CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on Calendar for Friday, August 9, 2013, Line 11, DEFENDANT THE CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT The motion to strike is granted as to all causes of action. As to the first prong of the anti-SLAPP analysis, Defendant has met its initial to show that Defendant's actions in the permitting process is protected activity under CCP §425.16(e). As to the second prong, Plaintiffs have not demonstrated a probability of prevailing on the merits. January 12, 1998, Plaintiffs signed a release of all claims existing or may exist pertaining to the facts set forth in Yee v. CCSF (case no. 980-740) or related to any transactions, occurrences, acts or omissions set forth or alleged in any of the pleading in the that case. Thus, Plaintiffs have not demonstrated that the first through fourth causes of action in the instant action do not relate to the settlement of claims in the prior action. (See Yee Decl., 12.) For the fifth and sixth causes of action, Plaintiffs' claims are barred by Gov. Code § 818.4. As the prevailing party, Defendant is entitled to recover his or her attorney's fees and costs from Plaintiffs. (CCP § 425.16(c)(1).) The Court awards $18,408 as reasonable fees/costs. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/PHA
